text: The laparoscopic view shows the sigmoid colon with a giant diverticulum, now located in the greater pelvis. Four trocars are used for the procedure, and the exploratory laparoscopy reveals the diverticulum's position.

The surgeon performs a lateral-to-medial approach on the sigmoid colon, initially detaching the descending colon. Blunt dissection and monopolar scissors are used to address signs of diverticulitis at the lateral diverticulum margin. The gonadal vessels and left ureter are identified and preserved. Inflammatory signs, including edema and erythema, are visible in the lateral wall of the diverticulum at the recto-sigmoid junction.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum to access the vascular structures, verifying the location of the left ureter. A 60mm Echelon stapler with a white cartridge is used for vascular ligation. The dissection of the recto-sigmoid junction is completed using ligature, followed by division with a 60mm Echelon stapler with a blue cartridge.

The surgical specimen is extracted through a five-centimeter incision using an Alexis wound retractor. A tension-free colocolonic anastomosis is performed with a 31mm circular EEA stapler. An intraoperative air leak test confirms the integrity of the anastomosis, and a drain is placed in the surgical site.
The pathology examination shows a giant colonic diverticulum with a fibrous chlorotic wall, displaying signs of acute and chronic inflammation, but no evidence of malignancy.
